title: The Macro Minute: Is the US economy being run too hot, Pt. II?

Happy Wednesday out 1342. It's your
skipper here, Darius Dale, to present
our macro minute for Wednesday, May
13th, 2026. As always, we'll start with
the executive summary from today's lead
up running note. So, let's dive right
in. Today's key macro question is, is
the US economy being run too hot, part
two?
The answer is yes, according to the
April PPI report, which was highly
supportive of our sticky inflation
theme. As we predicted over 2 years ago,
the structurally elevated bottom in the
core PPI cycle perfectly presaged
similar dynamics in core CBI and core
PCE.
Worse, core PPI is moonshotting to the
upside. Even worse, inflation is
profoundly misbehaving across a variety
of key metrics, despite what we view as
a transitory narrowing of the federal
budget deficit on an annualized fiscal
year-to-date basis.
If our math is correct, then ABA and
true interest expense will exacerbate
the US's current core inflation problem
and catalyze an incrementally and
perhaps destabilizing hawkish pivot in
the Fed's reaction function in the
coming months.
The Fed is lagging well behind its
central bank peers in terms of the
policy tightening that is priced in over
the next 12 months. SNB's got one hike
priced in, the Bank of England and the
Bank of Japan have three hikes priced
in, the ECB has three to four hikes
priced in, versus the Fed with only 0.5
hikes.
More hawkish inflation data would likely
induce a Fed catch-up trade that would
likely be dollar positive, well, not
likely, it would be dollar positive,
global liquidity negative, and
explicitly bearish for risk assets.
Uh amid the return of crowded bullish
positioning, uh the eventual reopening
of the Strait of Hormuz is now
officially at risk of being a
sell-the-news catalyst.
As always, I'll trans- I'll wrap up with
a question from our community. Uh so, uh
this one's titled uh take profits in AI
providers and rotate to AI doctors for
the next leg. They're quoting me on
that. I think for my Bloomberg segment.
Uh so, hey DD, uh is there a specific
list of stocks or uh sectors that you
feel will benefit the most? AI doctors
is fairly wide. It could be anything
from financials, retail, transport,
healthcare, even restaurants. Since
you're recommending rotation, I was
wondering if you favored one sector over
another. So, I'll start by saying, you
know, you guys know how I feel about
retail investors taking back to risk.
Do it at your own peril, but there's no
reason for you to do that if your goal
is to retire on time and come to it. If
your goal is to play Captain Hedge Fund,
then yeah, let's go. Let's Let's dive
right in. Uh from my perspective,
if you look at the sectors that have uh
lagged uh this throughout the duration
of this risk-on market regime, which is
well over a month old now, uh
the sectors that have lagged the most
are are two of the sectors that have
lagged the most are financials and
healthcare. Uh because again, the market
has basically rotated aggressively into
AI providers because they know that's
where the sales and earnings growth is
going to be from a near-term cash flow
and and and and and and and and and and
buyback generation perspective.
When you When the trader who moves is
open and assuming we don't get the spin
catch-up trade that would cause
volatility in financial markets, let's
put that to aside. When the trader who
moves dynamic is open, it's probably
likely it's highly likely in our opinion
that we're going to see a reversal of
such crowded both positioning into those
AI providers back into the AI doctors
because, you know, for a variety of
reasons, but we're already starting to
see uh the productivity enhancing
benefits of the technology as it
diffuses throughout the economy and we
think that is only going to accelerate
in the coming quarters and years and
catalyze a convergence in the
profitability, uh the the the the
productivity, uh the margins, and
ultimately the profitability and
valuations of companies that have lagged
behind uh the AI providers. Again,
they're transferring the same exact
technology that allows them to have
structurally elevated gross margins and
and and much higher rates of free cash
flow uh per share growth to the rest of
the busi- the rest of the economy. And
so, that transfer of technologies is
much like what we saw with we
transferred all of our technology to
China, is there's going to be a catch-up
uh in terms of the operating metrics for
those AI providers relative to the AI
beneficiaries. So, in our view, if you
look at what's lagged, it's the sectors
that have have that that have not, you
know, it's basically rotating out of the
things that would have
done really well in the AI beneficiary
trade. So, you think about financials,
you think about uh healthcare. Both of
those sectors have a lot of employee
compensation as a percent of revenue.
And you guys know
how we feel about the labor market from
a longer-term perspective in the context
of our jobless recovery theme. So, I
think that's pretty much it, but broadly
speaking, I don't think you need to take
sector risk here to pay the AI adopters
trade. You know, you can make a much
easier, much simpler trade where it
reduces your risk of being wrong by just
betting on global equities as opposed to
the Nasdaq 100 or even more more
narrowly
the Mac 7 or AI
you know, specific names. You think
about the DRAM players and the and the
and the energy providers to the AI
trade. You know, just bet on global
equities. We're going to see a
convergence between the median business
and the median businesses margins, the
median businesses productivity, the
median businesses profitability. And if
we're correct on this theme over a
multi-year time horizon, the median
businesses valuation relative to the
companies who are taking on a tremendous
amount of debt to raise a tremendous
amount of capex and ultimately may or
may not have the ROI that they need to
justify
such you know, what I would consider to
be an AI capex bubble in the coming
years. So, to me it's a very obvious
trade. We've seen this
movie play out before. US transferring
technology to China in early 2000s.
We've seen this movie play out before in
the 90s with the European convergence
trade when they launched the euro, you
saw the spreads with all the peripheral
countries in Europe narrow as their
currencies were being converted into the
euro on a multi-year time horizon basis.
That's essentially what we're calling
here today from a bottom-up from a micro
standpoint. So, we'll wrap it up there.
Darius still here presenting our macro
minute for Wednesday, May 13th, 2026.
